perturbation.original_text: Terms of payment will be 1/3 upon placement of order and 2/3 balance net thirty (30) days or as mutually agreed upon in writing between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Terms of payment will be 1/3 upon placement of order and 2/3 balance net sixty (60) days or as mutually agreed upon in writing between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms from net 30 to net 60 days for the balance due could create an issue under the Colorado Wage Claim Act if this is construed as an arrangement for labor. Colorado law mandates prompt payment of wages.
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado Revised Statutes 8-4-109 specifies requirements for timely wage payments. Extending the payment term to 60 days may violate the requirement for timely payment, particularly if the distributor is considered an employee or contractor performing labor, which the statute aims to protect.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'net thirty (30) days' to 'net sixty (60) days' for payment terms directly contradicts the obligation for timely wage payments as described in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. While specific snippets of law were not provided to illustrate the exact legal phrasing of 'timely payment,' the core principle derived from the explanations is that extending payment terms can violate wage payment laws, especially if construed as labor compensation. The modified text introduces a potential non-compliance with wage laws, leading to a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall be two (2) years from the Effective Date with automatic annual renewals thereafter provided either party does not provide sixty (60) days notice of termination prior to the renewal date or the Agreement is not otherwise terminated as set forth in Section 8.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall be two (2) years from the Effective Date with automatic annual renewals thereafter provided either party does not provide thirty (30) days notice of termination prior to the renewal date or the Agreement is not otherwise terminated as set forth in Section 8.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 60 days to 30 days could be problematic if it unreasonably restricts a party's ability to terminate the agreement and conflicts with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Colorado law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 and its interpretation in Colorado law (which follows the restatement) imposes a duty of good faith and fair dealing in the performance and enforcement of contracts. A 30-day notice period for termination, depending on the circumstances (e.g., significant investments, reliance on the agreement), could be deemed to violate this duty if it doesn't allow a reasonable opportunity to mitigate damages or wind down operations, especially because the original agreement called for a 60 day period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a 60-day termination notice, while the changed text reduces this to 30 days. The Law Explanation suggests that this reduction could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Colorado law, especially if it doesn't allow a reasonable opportunity to mitigate damages or wind down operations. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "good faith" as encompassing honest dealing, faithful performance of duties, and observance of fair dealing standards. Reducing the notice period could be interpreted as a failure to observe fair dealing standards if it prejudices one party, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination, ENVISION shall have eighteen (18) months to exhaust any inventories, packaging and advertising materials bearing the "SierraSil" trademark and SIERRA shall have first option to buy back any inventory at ENVISION's net purchase price.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination, ENVISION shall have six (6) months to exhaust any inventories, packaging and advertising materials bearing the "SierraSil" trademark and SIERRA shall have first option to buy back any inventory at ENVISION's net purchase price.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the sell-off period post-termination from 18 months to 6 months may be unreasonably short. It is possible that a court would find a 6-month sell off period to be inadequate to exhaust inventory which may violate Colorado's Uniform Commercial Code regarding reasonable notification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Uniform Commercial Code - Reasonable Notification
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado Revised Statutes Section 4-2-309 requires 'reasonable notification' when an agreement is terminated. What constitutes reasonable notification depends on the circumstances. Reducing the sell-off period to six months may not give Envision a reasonable opportunity to sell off existing inventory. Depending on the volume of inventory, it could be viewed as commercially unreasonable and in violation of the statute's implied requirement for reasonable notification of termination affecting inventory disposal.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the sell-off period from 18 months to 6 months, potentially violating Colorado's Uniform Commercial Code regarding reasonable notification as detailed in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the possibility that a court would find the reduced period inadequate, suggesting non-compliance with the implicit requirement for reasonable notification of termination affecting inventory disposal, as referenced in Colorado Revised Statutes Section 4-2-309 according to the Law Explanation. Since the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states that a legal contradiction exists when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, and the altered text reduces a deadline that may not provide 'reasonable notification,' a contradiction exists.
